title: Act or Leave Alone
intro: You hold a flexi-cap SIP for years. A fresh analysis shows it has quietly behaved like a large-cap fund. What now?

nodes.text: Your fund’s large-cap weight has barely moved through a crash and a bull market. Mid and small sleeves stay thin. Your first instinct?

choices.label: Sell everything tomorrow and switch to a hot multi-cap
Pause and verify: tax, goals, and whether the drift is real for MY folio
Ignore forever — analysis always causes harm

nodes.text: STCG/LTCG, exit loads, and timing risk hit at once. You may have fixed a style mismatch by creating a tax and behaviour problem. Want a calmer path?

choices.label: Back up — verify first, then adjust on the margin

nodes.text: Blind inaction is not the same as patience. If the fund no longer matches the job you hired it for, you still need a plan — just not a panicked one.

choices.label: Verify holdings vs my intent, then decide

nodes.text: Holdings confirm large-cap bias. Your goal was ‘true diversified flex across market caps.’ Sensible next move?

choices.label: Redirect new SIPs / fresh money toward a fund that matches the job; leave old units unless tax/plan says otherwise
Day-trade the switch across five funds this week based on last quarter’s winners

ending.principle: Read the finding; resist restless churn. Correct with patience — often on fresh investments.
ending.debrief: There is a difference between acting on every new article and noticing a lasting mandate mismatch. Knowing what you own is most of the work; the rest can be done calmly, one fresh investment at a time.

ending.principle: The impulse to act on every new finding has harmed more investors than many market falls.
ending.debrief: Style awareness is useful; hyperactivity is not. Prefer deliberate reallocation over tax-blind, winner-chasing exits.
